sentences.base_language: Andrés bought a guitar three weeks ago and has been practicing every day.
sentences.target_language: Andrés compró una guitarra hace tres semanas y lleva practicando todos los días.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lleva + gerund** expresses an ongoing action with duration: lleva practicando = he's been practicing (and still is).
llevo aprendiendo español seis meses = I've been learning Spanish for six months.

sentences.base_language: He's learning a few basic chords — C, G, and D.
sentences.target_language: Está aprendiendo unos acordes básicos: do, sol y re.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Está aprendiendo** is the present progressive (estar + gerund), used to emphasize an action happening right now. Spanish uses it less than English — for habits or ongoing situations, the simple present is more natural:
aprendo guitarra = I'm learning guitar / I learn guitar.

sentences.base_language: His fingers hurt a little from pressing on the strings.
sentences.target_language: Le duelen un poco los dedos de tanto presionar las cuerdas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le duelen** = his fingers hurt him — **doler** (to hurt) works like **gustar**: the thing that hurts is the grammatical subject.
**Le duele la cabeza** (his/her head hurts), **le duelen los pies** (his/her feet hurt) — verb agrees with what hurts, not the person.
**De tanto + infinitive** = from so much (doing something): **de tanto practicar** (from practicing so much).

sentences.base_language: 'Is this normal?' he asks his friend Julia, who has been playing for years.
sentences.target_language: —¿Es normal? —le pregunta a su amiga Julia, que lleva años tocando.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tocar** la guitarra (not jugar) is the correct verb for playing an instrument.
**jugar** = for games and sports: jugar al fútbol, jugar ajedrez
**tocar** = for instruments: tocar el piano, tocar la batería

sentences.base_language: 'Totally normal! You're developing calluses. It gets easier, I promise.'
sentences.target_language: —¡Totalmente normal! Estás formando callos. Se pone más fácil, te lo juro.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estás formando** = you are developing — present progressive for something happening right now.
**Se pone más fácil** = it gets easier — **ponerse** + adjective = to become/get (for changes): **se pone triste** (gets sad), **se pone difícil** (gets difficult).
**Te lo juro** = I promise/I swear to you — **lo** refers to the statement just made (neuter object pronoun).

sentences.base_language: Andrés practices for an hour every evening after dinner.
sentences.target_language: Andrés practica una hora todas las noches después de cenar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Practica** = (he) practices — **practicar** is a regular -ar verb in meaning, but has a spelling change: **c → qu** before **e** (in the preterite: **practiqué**).
**Todas las noches** = every evening/night — **todas** (feminine plural) agrees with **noches**.
**Después de + infinitive** = after doing something: **después de cenar** (after having dinner), **después de trabajar** (after working).

sentences.base_language: Little by little, his chord changes are getting smoother.
sentences.target_language: Poco a poco, los cambios de acordes le salen más fluidos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Poco a poco** = little by little — a fixed adverbial phrase.
**Le salen** = come out for him / turn out for him — **salir** used idiomatically: **le sale bien** (it comes out well for him), **le salen fluidos** (they come out smooth).
**Los cambios de acordes** = chord changes — **cambio** (change) + **de** + **acordes** (chords).
**Fluidos** = smooth/fluent — adjective agreeing with **cambios** (masculine plural).

sentences.base_language: One evening, he finally plays his first complete song from start to finish.
sentences.target_language: Una noche, por fin toca su primera canción completa de principio a fin.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Por fin** = finally/at last — a common adverbial expression.
**Toca** = (he) plays — **tocar** for instruments (not **jugar**).
**Su primera canción** — **primera** (first, feminine) agrees with **canción** (feminine).
**De principio a fin** = from start to finish — a fixed phrase. Also: **de cabo a rabo** (from head to tail — more colloquial).

sentences.base_language: He plays it twice and grins from ear to ear.
sentences.target_language: La toca dos veces y sonríe de oreja a oreja.
sentences.grammarNote:
**La toca** = he plays it — **la** is the direct object pronoun replacing **la canción** (feminine).
Object pronouns go before conjugated verbs in Spanish: **la toca** (he plays it), not *toca la*.
**Dos veces** = twice — **una vez** (once), **dos veces** (twice), **tres veces** (three times).
**De oreja a oreja** = from ear to ear — idiomatic expression for a big grin.

sentences.base_language: He texts Julia: 'I did it!! I just played the whole song!'
sentences.target_language: Le manda un mensaje a Julia: «¡Lo logré!! ¡Acabo de tocar la canción entera!»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le manda un mensaje** = he sends her a message — **le** is the indirect object pronoun (to her).
**Lo logré** = I did it / I achieved it — **lograr** (to achieve/manage to do): **lo logré** (I achieved it).
**Acabo de + infinitive** = I just did something — **acabar de** expresses a very recent action: **acabo de comer** (I just ate), **acaba de llegar** (she just arrived).
**La canción entera** = the whole song — **entero/a** (whole/entire) follows the noun.

sentences.base_language: She replies with a row of guitar emojis and 'I told you so!'
sentences.target_language: Ella responde con un montón de emojis de guitarra y: «¡Te lo dije!»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Responde** = (she) replies — **responder** is a regular -er verb.
**Un montón de** = a lot of / a bunch of — colloquial intensifier: **un montón de mensajes** (a ton of messages).
**Te lo dije** = I told you so — **te** (to you) + **lo** (it, neuter) + **dije** (preterite of **decir**).
**Dije** is from the highly irregular **decir** preterite: **dije, dijiste, dijo, dijimos, dijisteis, dijeron**.
